primary_factors: Rockford Public Schools has an explicit pre-announcement (3/11) that school WILL be in session Fri 3/13—strong decision-signal that lowers full-closure odds absent a major overnight change

Forecast is a wind/rain/visibility event (High Wind Watch; heavy showers/low visibility 06:00–10:00) with essentially 0" snow accumulation—Michigan districts usually stay open unless impacts are confirmed
Primary closure pathway is wind-driven outages/debris and/or persistently very low visibility during bus routes; these are plausible but not yet confirmed
Infrastructure/plow status is effectively a non-factor (roads currently clear; no plowable snow expected); risk is more about ponding/hydroplaning, debris, and isolated bridge slick spots
Power situation: Kent County outages are present and trending up but still low percentage (<1%); not enough alone to force closure yet
decision_rationale:
Cross-check of inputs shows no major conflicts on the big picture: all experts agree this is NOT a snow-accumulation day (modeled snow ~0"; temps mainly mid-30s at commute) and the real hazards are high wind, heavy rain/ponding, and sharp visibility drops during 6–10 AM. The only meaningful tension is how strongly to translate ‘high crash-risk morning’ into a full-district closure. In West Michigan, districts commonly attempt to operate through rain/wind with mitigation (slower buses, route adjustments) unless (1) wind gusts are repeatedly ≥45–50 mph at bus time, (2) visibility stays ≤1 mile across much of the district, or (3) outages/debris become widespread.

News intelligence is the key tiebreaker here: Rockford Public Schools already told families (3/11) that Friday will be in session, and a fresh scan found no newer override; neighboring districts also show no posted weather closures/delays at this time, and closings aggregators show none. That strongly reduces the probability of a full ‘snow day’ cancellation. 

What could make this forecast wrong (and push probability up fast) is a late-breaking overnight wind verification near the high-end of the watch (damage/debris, dark signals) or an outage surge that affects facilities/major corridors before the 5:30–6:00 AM decision. Power data shows Kent County outages increasing (still low %, but not zero), so the closure tail risk is real—but not the base case.

Net: I expect Rockford is more likely OPEN (possibly with heightened monitoring) than fully CLOSED. If administrators choose disruption, a 2-hour DELAY is a more probable tool than a full cancellation given the worst window is concentrated in the morning.

collaboration.summary: Agents reached consensus after 1 round(s) with 9.0% spread. Average probability: 23%.
